Shrine |画像 = [[File:Morosugi-jinja01s4592.jpg|280px]]
Torii and haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|27|38.34|N|134|52|30.43|E|region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Morosugi Shrine}} |祭神 = Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Three-bay irimoya-zukuri |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Mid-October (Autumn festival) |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Morosugi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Morosugi Shrine''' (もろすぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Izushi-chō Uchimachi, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is located at the northern foot of Ariko-yama, east of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}. === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deity is the following one kamiPrecinct information board.: * '''Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami''' (たじまもろすくのかみ) *: In the '{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}},' the name is written as "Tajima-Morosuku," and in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}},' as "Tajima-Morosuke." Both texts identify this deity as a child of {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hihoko). The shrine name "Morosugi" is said to derive from "Morosuku (Morosuke)"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. As noted above, the enshrined deity is regarded as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, the deity who opened up the Izushi area. Throughout the Izushi region, in addition to Morosugi Shrine, shrines of the Ame-no-Hiboko lineage are widely distributed, including {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Miyauchi), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mi-Izushi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Kirino), {{ill|lt=Nakashima Shrine|Q11364695|WD=Q11364695|1=Nakashima Shrine}} (Toyooka, Miyake), and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sugi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Araki){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown. According to shrine tradition, the shrine was originally located in Mizukami Village (present-day Toyooka, Izushi-chō Mizukami), near Izushi Shrine, and was transferred to its present location in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Since Tajima-Morosuku (Tajima-Morosuke), from whom the name "Morosugi" derives, appears in both the 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' and is moreover identified as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, Morosugi Shrine is considered to have been established in ancient times{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. The Mizukami area, said to be the shrine's original site, remains part of Morosugi Shrine's parishioner territory to this day, and during festivals a portable shrine procession (mikoshi togyo) is conducted all the way to Mizukami{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}{{Sfn|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is recorded as "Morosugi Shrine / Morosugi Shrine" under {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} {{ill|lt=Izushi District|Q11395767|WD=Q11395767|1=Izushi District, Hyōgo}}, and is listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Subsequent history is not well documented, but as mentioned above, in Tenshō 2 (1574), when the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|WD=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} relocated from {{ill|lt=Konosumi-yama Castle|Q11545747|WD=Q11545747|1=Konosumiyama Castle}} to {{ill|lt=Ariko-yama Castle|Q11516348|WD=Q11516348|1=Arikoyama Castle}}, Morosugi Shrine was also transferred from Mizukami Village to its present location below the castle{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was enshrined north of the yamato-no-kuruwa (mountain villa enclosure) of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}{{Sfn|内町（平凡社）|1999年}}, and received reverence from the successive castle lords of the {{ill|lt=Koide clan|Q11459112|WD=Q11459112|1=Koide clan (Negi Daibu)}}, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira clan|Q1141077|WD=Q1141077|1=Matsudaira clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Sengoku clan|Q11378184|WD=Q11378184|1=Sengoku clan}}, who provided for shrine construction and donations of offerings. During the Sengoku clan's tenure as castle lords, it was customary for the lord himself to visit Morosugi Shrine upon returning from {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the Edo period, the shrine was also known as "Morosugi Daimyōjin"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and on April 21 of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. In the interim, the shrine buildings were destroyed by fire in Meiji 9 ({{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}}), but were rebuilt in Meiji 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}). === Precinct === * Honden - Three bays on the front, two bays on the rear, and two bays on the sides, in {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, with a chidori-hafu (cusped gable) on the front. The roof is clad in copper sheeting. * Haiden - irimoya-zukuri style with a tiled roof{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. * Mikoshi storehouse File:Morosugi-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Morosugi-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden === Auxiliary shrines === * Kawakami Shrine - Deity: Seoritsuhime-no-Kami * Tenjin Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shanichi Shrine * Nitta Shrine * Mihashira Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Inari Shrine * Ōkuni Shrine * Inari Shrine === Festivals === * Annual festival (Sunday before October 15) *: Formerly held on September 9, and later on October 15{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)|Q11587232|WD=Q11587232|lt=Isobe Shrine|1=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ifukube Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, a "danjiri matsuri" is held in which approximately 20 {{ill|lt=danjiri|Q11271630|WD=Q11271630|1=danjiri}} are crashed into each other in front of Izushi Castle in a fighting festival. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 '''Access''' * Approximately 4 minutes on foot southeast from {{ill|lt=Shinkoro|Q11637916|WD=Q11637916|1=Shinkorō}} in central Izushi '''Vicinity''' * {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Shrine |画像 = [[File:Morosugi-jinja01s4592.jpg|280px]]
Torii and haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|27|38.34|N|134|52|30.43|E|region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Morosugi Shrine}} |祭神 = Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Three-bay irimoya-zukuri |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Mid-October (Autumn festival) |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Morosugi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Morosugi Shrine''' (もろすぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Izushi-chō Uchimachi, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is located at the northern foot of Ariko-yama, east of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}. === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deity is the following one kamiPrecinct information board.: * '''Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami''' (たじまもろすくのかみ) *: In the '{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}},' the name is written as "Tajima-Morosuku," and in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}},' as "Tajima-Morosuke." Both texts identify this deity as a child of {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hihoko). The shrine name "Morosugi" is said to derive from "Morosuku (Morosuke)"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. As noted above, the enshrined deity is regarded as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, the deity who opened up the Izushi area. Throughout the Izushi region, in addition to Morosugi Shrine, shrines of the Ame-no-Hiboko lineage are widely distributed, including {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Miyauchi), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mi-Izushi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Kirino), {{ill|lt=Nakashima Shrine|Q11364695|WD=Q11364695|1=Nakashima Shrine}} (Toyooka, Miyake), and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sugi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Araki){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown. According to shrine tradition, the shrine was originally located in Mizukami Village (present-day Toyooka, Izushi-chō Mizukami), near Izushi Shrine, and was transferred to its present location in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Since Tajima-Morosuku (Tajima-Morosuke), from whom the name "Morosugi" derives, appears in both the 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' and is moreover identified as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, Morosugi Shrine is considered to have been established in ancient times{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. The Mizukami area, said to be the shrine's original site, remains part of Morosugi Shrine's parishioner territory to this day, and during festivals a portable shrine procession (mikoshi togyo) is conducted all the way to Mizukami{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}{{Sfn|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is recorded as "Morosugi Shrine / Morosugi Shrine" under {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} {{ill|lt=Izushi District|Q11395767|WD=Q11395767|1=Izushi District, Hyōgo}}, and is listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Subsequent history is not well documented, but as mentioned above, in Tenshō 2 (1574), when the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|WD=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} relocated from {{ill|lt=Konosumi-yama Castle|Q11545747|WD=Q11545747|1=Konosumiyama Castle}} to {{ill|lt=Ariko-yama Castle|Q11516348|WD=Q11516348|1=Arikoyama Castle}}, Morosugi Shrine was also transferred from Mizukami Village to its present location below the castle{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was enshrined north of the yamato-no-kuruwa (mountain villa enclosure) of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}{{Sfn|内町（平凡社）|1999年}}, and received reverence from the successive castle lords of the {{ill|lt=Koide clan|Q11459112|WD=Q11459112|1=Koide clan (Negi Daibu)}}, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira clan|Q1141077|WD=Q1141077|1=Matsudaira clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Sengoku clan|Q11378184|WD=Q11378184|1=Sengoku clan}}, who provided for shrine construction and donations of offerings. During the Sengoku clan's tenure as castle lords, it was customary for the lord himself to visit Morosugi Shrine upon returning from {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the Edo period, the shrine was also known as "Morosugi Daimyōjin"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and on April 21 of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. In the interim, the shrine buildings were destroyed by fire in Meiji 9 ({{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}}), but were rebuilt in Meiji 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}). === Precinct === * Honden - Three bays on the front, two bays on the rear, and two bays on the sides, in {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, with a chidori-hafu (cusped gable) on the front. The roof is clad in copper sheeting. * Haiden - irimoya-zukuri style with a tiled roof{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. * Mikoshi storehouse File:Morosugi-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Morosugi-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden === Auxiliary shrines === * Kawakami Shrine - Deity: Seoritsuhime-no-Kami * Tenjin Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shanichi Shrine * Nitta Shrine * Mihashira Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Inari Shrine * Ōkuni Shrine * Inari Shrine === Festivals === * Annual festival (Sunday before October 15) *: Formerly held on September 9, and later on October 15{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)|Q11587232|WD=Q11587232|lt=Isobe Shrine|1=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ifukube Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, a "danjiri matsuri" is held in which approximately 20 {{ill|lt=danjiri|Q11271630|WD=Q11271630|1=danjiri}} are crashed into each other in front of Izushi Castle in a fighting festival. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 '''Access''' * Approximately 4 minutes on foot southeast from {{ill|lt=Shinkoro|Q11637916|WD=Q11637916|1=Shinkorō}} in central Izushi '''Vicinity''' * {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Meiji Jinja Shiryō Compilation Office, ed.|author=|year=1912|chapter=Morosugi Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Jinja Shiryō Compilation Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|明治神社誌料|1912年}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/387}} 'Meiji Jinja Shiryō: Fuken Gōsha, Volume 1'] (National Diet Library Digital Collection), see frames 387-388. * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society, ed.|author={{ill|lt=Kuwahara Kiminori|Q126707715|WD=Q126707715|1=Tadanori Kuwabara}}|chapter=Morosugi Shrine|year=1984|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 19|publisher={{ill|lt=Kōgakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei According to shrine tradition, the shrine was originally located in Mizukami Village (present-day Toyooka, Izushi-chō Mizukami), near Izushi Shrine, and was transferred to its present location in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Since Tajima-Morosuku (Tajima-Morosuke), from whom the name "Morosugi" derives, appears in both the 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' and is moreover identified as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, Morosugi Shrine is considered to have been established in ancient times{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. The Mizukami area, said to be the shrine's original site, remains part of Morosugi Shrine's parishioner territory to this day, and during festivals a portable shrine procession (mikoshi togyo) is conducted all the way to Mizukami{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}{{Sfn|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is recorded as "Morosugi Shrine / Morosugi Shrine" under {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} {{ill|lt=Izushi District|Q11395767|WD=Q11395767|1=Izushi District, Hyōgo}}, and is listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Subsequent history is not well documented, but as mentioned above, in Tenshō 2 (1574), when the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|WD=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} relocated from {{ill|lt=Konosumi-yama Castle|Q11545747|WD=Q11545747|1=Konosumiyama Castle}} to {{ill|lt=Ariko-yama Castle|Q11516348|WD=Q11516348|1=Arikoyama Castle}}, Morosugi Shrine was also transferred from Mizukami Village to its present location below the castle{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was enshrined north of the yamato-no-kuruwa (mountain villa enclosure) of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}{{Sfn|内町（平凡社）|1999年}}, and received reverence from the successive castle lords of the {{ill|lt=Koide clan|Q11459112|WD=Q11459112|1=Koide clan (Negi Daibu)}}, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira clan|Q1141077|WD=Q1141077|1=Matsudaira clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Sengoku clan|Q11378184|WD=Q11378184|1=Sengoku clan}}, who provided for shrine construction and donations of offerings. During the Sengoku clan's tenure as castle lords, it was customary for the lord himself to visit Morosugi Shrine upon returning from {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the Edo period, the shrine was also known as "Morosugi Daimyōjin"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and on April 21 of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. In the interim, the shrine buildings were destroyed by fire in Meiji 9 ({{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}}), but were rebuilt in Meiji 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}). === Precinct === * Honden - Three bays on the front, two bays on the rear, and two bays on the sides, in {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, with a chidori-hafu (cusped gable) on the front. The roof is clad in copper sheeting. * Haiden - irimoya-zukuri style with a tiled roof{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. * Mikoshi storehouse File:Morosugi-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Morosugi-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden === Auxiliary shrines === * Kawakami Shrine - Deity: Seoritsuhime-no-Kami * Tenjin Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shanichi Shrine * Nitta Shrine * Mihashira Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Inari Shrine * Ōkuni Shrine * Inari Shrine === Festivals === * Annual festival (Sunday before October 15) *: Formerly held on September 9, and later on October 15{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)|Q11587232|WD=Q11587232|lt=Isobe Shrine|1=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ifukube Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, a "danjiri matsuri" is held in which approximately 20 {{ill|lt=danjiri|Q11271630|WD=Q11271630|1=danjiri}} are crashed into each other in front of Izushi Castle in a fighting festival. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 '''Access''' * Approximately 4 minutes on foot southeast from {{ill|lt=Shinkoro|Q11637916|WD=Q11637916|1=Shinkorō}} in central Izushi '''Vicinity''' * {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Three-bay irimoya-zukuri |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Mid-October (Autumn festival) |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Morosugi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Morosugi Shrine''' (もろすぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Izushi-chō Uchimachi, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is located at the northern foot of Ariko-yama, east of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}. === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deity is the following one kamiPrecinct information board.: * '''Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami''' (たじまもろすくのかみ) *: In the '{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}},' the name is written as "Tajima-Morosuku," and in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}},' as "Tajima-Morosuke." Both texts identify this deity as a child of {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hihoko). The shrine name "Morosugi" is said to derive from "Morosuku (Morosuke)"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. As noted above, the enshrined deity is regarded as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, the deity who opened up the Izushi area. Throughout the Izushi region, in addition to Morosugi Shrine, shrines of the Ame-no-Hiboko lineage are widely distributed, including {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Miyauchi), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mi-Izushi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Kirino), {{ill|lt=Nakashima Shrine|Q11364695|WD=Q11364695|1=Nakashima Shrine}} (Toyooka, Miyake), and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sugi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Araki){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown. According to shrine tradition, the shrine was originally located in Mizukami Village (present-day Toyooka, Izushi-chō Mizukami), near Izushi Shrine, and was transferred to its present location in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Since Tajima-Morosuku (Tajima-Morosuke), from whom the name "Morosugi" derives, appears in both the 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' and is moreover identified as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, Morosugi Shrine is considered to have been established in ancient times{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. The Mizukami area, said to be the shrine's original site, remains part of Morosugi Shrine's parishioner territory to this day, and during festivals a portable shrine procession (mikoshi togyo) is conducted all the way to Mizukami{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}{{Sfn|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is recorded as "Morosugi Shrine / Morosugi Shrine" under {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} {{ill|lt=Izushi District|Q11395767|WD=Q11395767|1=Izushi District, Hyōgo}}, and is listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Subsequent history is not well documented, but as mentioned above, in Tenshō 2 (1574), when the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|WD=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} relocated from {{ill|lt=Konosumi-yama Castle|Q11545747|WD=Q11545747|1=Konosumiyama Castle}} to {{ill|lt=Ariko-yama Castle|Q11516348|WD=Q11516348|1=Arikoyama Castle}}, Morosugi Shrine was also transferred from Mizukami Village to its present location below the castle{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the Sengoku clan's tenure as castle lords, it was customary for the lord himself to visit Morosugi Shrine upon returning from {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県豊岡市出石町内町28', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Morosugi-jinja}} [[JA:諸杉神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11632102}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Morosugi Shrine |画像 = [[File:Morosugi-jinja01s4592.jpg|280px]]
Torii and haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|27|38.34|N|134|52|30.43|E|region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Morosugi Shrine}} |祭神 = Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Three-bay irimoya-zukuri |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Mid-October (Autumn festival) |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Morosugi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Morosugi Shrine''' (もろすぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Izushi-chō Uchimachi, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is located at the northern foot of Ariko-yama, east of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}. === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deity is the following one kamiPrecinct information board.: * '''Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami''' (たじまもろすくのかみ) *: In the '{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}},' the name is written as "Tajima-Morosuku," and in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}},' as "Tajima-Morosuke." Both texts identify this deity as a child of {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hihoko). The shrine name "Morosugi" is said to derive from "Morosuku (Morosuke)"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. As noted above, the enshrined deity is regarded as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, the deity who opened up the Izushi area. Throughout the Izushi region, in addition to Morosugi Shrine, shrines of the Ame-no-Hiboko lineage are widely distributed, including {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Miyauchi), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mi-Izushi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Kirino), {{ill|lt=Nakashima Shrine|Q11364695|WD=Q11364695|1=Nakashima Shrine}} (Toyooka, Miyake), and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sugi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Araki){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown. According to shrine tradition, the shrine was originally located in Mizukami Village (present-day Toyooka, Izushi-chō Mizukami), near Izushi Shrine, and was transferred to its present location in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Since Tajima-Morosuku (Tajima-Morosuke), from whom the name "Morosugi" derives, appears in both the 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' and is moreover identified as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, Morosugi Shrine is considered to have been established in ancient times{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. The Mizukami area, said to be the shrine's original site, remains part of Morosugi Shrine's parishioner territory to this day, and during festivals a portable shrine procession (mikoshi togyo) is conducted all the way to Mizukami{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}{{Sfn|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is recorded as "Morosugi Shrine / Morosugi Shrine" under {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} {{ill|lt=Izushi District|Q11395767|WD=Q11395767|1=Izushi District, Hyōgo}}, and is listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Subsequent history is not well documented, but as mentioned above, in Tenshō 2 (1574), when the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|WD=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} relocated from {{ill|lt=Konosumi-yama Castle|Q11545747|WD=Q11545747|1=Konosumiyama Castle}} to {{ill|lt=Ariko-yama Castle|Q11516348|WD=Q11516348|1=Arikoyama Castle}}, Morosugi Shrine was also transferred from Mizukami Village to its present location below the castle{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was enshrined north of the yamato-no-kuruwa (mountain villa enclosure) of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}{{Sfn|内町（平凡社）|1999年}}, and received reverence from the successive castle lords of the {{ill|lt=Koide clan|Q11459112|WD=Q11459112|1=Koide clan (Negi Daibu)}}, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira clan|Q1141077|WD=Q1141077|1=Matsudaira clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Sengoku clan|Q11378184|WD=Q11378184|1=Sengoku clan}}, who provided for shrine construction and donations of offerings. During the Sengoku clan's tenure as castle lords, it was customary for the lord himself to visit Morosugi Shrine upon returning from {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the Edo period, the shrine was also known as "Morosugi Daimyōjin"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and on April 21 of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. In the interim, the shrine buildings were destroyed by fire in Meiji 9 ({{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}}), but were rebuilt in Meiji 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}). === Precinct === * Honden - Three bays on the front, two bays on the rear, and two bays on the sides, in {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, with a chidori-hafu (cusped gable) on the front. The roof is clad in copper sheeting. * Haiden - irimoya-zukuri style with a tiled roof{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. * Mikoshi storehouse File:Morosugi-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Morosugi-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden === Auxiliary shrines === * Kawakami Shrine - Deity: Seoritsuhime-no-Kami * Tenjin Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shanichi Shrine * Nitta Shrine * Mihashira Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Inari Shrine * Ōkuni Shrine * Inari Shrine === Festivals === * Annual festival (Sunday before October 15) *: Formerly held on September 9, and later on October 15{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)|Q11587232|WD=Q11587232|lt=Isobe Shrine|1=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ifukube Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, a "danjiri matsuri" is held in which approximately 20 {{ill|lt=danjiri|Q11271630|WD=Q11271630|1=danjiri}} are crashed into each other in front of Izushi Castle in a fighting festival. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 '''Access''' * Approximately 4 minutes on foot southeast from {{ill|lt=Shinkoro|Q11637916|WD=Q11637916|1=Shinkorō}} in central Izushi '''Vicinity''' * {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Fuken Gōsha, Volume 1'] (National Diet Library Digital Collection), see frames 387-388. * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society, ed.|author={{ill|lt=Kuwahara Shrine |画像 = [[File:Morosugi-jinja01s4592.jpg|280px]]
Torii and haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|27|38.34|N|134|52|30.43|E|region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Morosugi Shrine}} |祭神 = Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Three-bay irimoya-zukuri |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Mid-October (Autumn festival) |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Morosugi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Morosugi Shrine''' (もろすぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Izushi-chō Uchimachi, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is located at the northern foot of Ariko-yama, east of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}. === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deity is the following one kamiPrecinct information board.: * '''Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami''' (たじまもろすくのかみ) *: In the '{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}},' the name is written as "Tajima-Morosuku," and in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}},' as "Tajima-Morosuke." Both texts identify this deity as a child of {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hihoko). The shrine name "Morosugi" is said to derive from "Morosuku (Morosuke)"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. As noted above, the enshrined deity is regarded as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, the deity who opened up the Izushi area. Throughout the Izushi region, in addition to Morosugi Shrine, shrines of the Ame-no-Hiboko lineage are widely distributed, including {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Miyauchi), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mi-Izushi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Kirino), {{ill|lt=Nakashima Shrine|Q11364695|WD=Q11364695|1=Nakashima Shrine}} (Toyooka, Miyake), and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sugi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Araki){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown. According to shrine tradition, the shrine was originally located in Mizukami Village (present-day Toyooka, Izushi-chō Mizukami), near Izushi Shrine, and was transferred to its present location in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Since Tajima-Morosuku (Tajima-Morosuke), from whom the name "Morosugi" derives, appears in both the 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' and is moreover identified as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, Morosugi Shrine is considered to have been established in ancient times{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. The Mizukami area, said to be the shrine's original site, remains part of Morosugi Shrine's parishioner territory to this day, and during festivals a portable shrine procession (mikoshi togyo) is conducted all the way to Mizukami{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}{{Sfn|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is recorded as "Morosugi Shrine / Morosugi Shrine" under {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} {{ill|lt=Izushi District|Q11395767|WD=Q11395767|1=Izushi District, Hyōgo}}, and is listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Subsequent history is not well documented, but as mentioned above, in Tenshō 2 (1574), when the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|WD=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} relocated from {{ill|lt=Konosumi-yama Castle|Q11545747|WD=Q11545747|1=Konosumiyama Castle}} to {{ill|lt=Ariko-yama Castle|Q11516348|WD=Q11516348|1=Arikoyama Castle}}, Morosugi Shrine was also transferred from Mizukami Village to its present location below the castle{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was enshrined north of the yamato-no-kuruwa (mountain villa enclosure) of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}{{Sfn|内町（平凡社）|1999年}}, and received reverence from the successive castle lords of the {{ill|lt=Koide clan|Q11459112|WD=Q11459112|1=Koide clan (Negi Daibu)}}, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira clan|Q1141077|WD=Q1141077|1=Matsudaira clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Sengoku clan|Q11378184|WD=Q11378184|1=Sengoku clan}}, who provided for shrine construction and donations of offerings. During the Sengoku clan's tenure as castle lords, it was customary for the lord himself to visit Morosugi Shrine upon returning from {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the Edo period, the shrine was also known as "Morosugi Daimyōjin"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and on April 21 of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. In the interim, the shrine buildings were destroyed by fire in Meiji 9 ({{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}}), but were rebuilt in Meiji 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}). === Precinct === * Honden - Three bays on the front, two bays on the rear, and two bays on the sides, in {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, with a chidori-hafu (cusped gable) on the front. The roof is clad in copper sheeting. * Haiden - irimoya-zukuri style with a tiled roof{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. * Mikoshi storehouse File:Morosugi-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Morosugi-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden === Auxiliary shrines === * Kawakami Shrine - Deity: Seoritsuhime-no-Kami * Tenjin Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shanichi Shrine * Nitta Shrine * Mihashira Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Inari Shrine * Ōkuni Shrine * Inari Shrine === Festivals === * Annual festival (Sunday before October 15) *: Formerly held on September 9, and later on October 15{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)|Q11587232|WD=Q11587232|lt=Isobe Shrine|1=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ifukube Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, a "danjiri matsuri" is held in which approximately 20 {{ill|lt=danjiri|Q11271630|WD=Q11271630|1=danjiri}} are crashed into each other in front of Izushi Castle in a fighting festival. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 '''Access''' * Approximately 4 minutes on foot southeast from {{ill|lt=Shinkoro|Q11637916|WD=Q11637916|1=Shinkorō}} in central Izushi '''Vicinity''' * {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Meiji Jinja Shiryō Compilation Office, ed.|author=|year=1912|chapter=Morosugi Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Jinja Shiryō Compilation Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|明治神社誌料|1912年}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/387}} 'Meiji Jinja Shiryō: Fuken Gōsha, Volume 1'] (National Diet Library Digital Collection), see frames 387-388. * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society, ed.|author={{ill|lt=Kuwahara Kiminori|Q126707715|WD=Q126707715|1=Tadanori Kuwabara}}|chapter=Morosugi Shrine|year=1984|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 19|publisher={{ill|lt=Kōgakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei As noted above, the enshrined deity is regarded as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, the deity who opened up the Izushi area. Throughout the Izushi region, in addition to Morosugi Shrine, shrines of the Ame-no-Hiboko lineage are widely distributed, including {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Miyauchi), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mi-Izushi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Kirino), {{ill|lt=Nakashima Shrine|Q11364695|WD=Q11364695|1=Nakashima Shrine}} (Toyooka, Miyake), and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sugi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Araki){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown. According to shrine tradition, the shrine was originally located in Mizukami Village (present-day Toyooka, Izushi-chō Mizukami), near Izushi Shrine, and was transferred to its present location in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Since Tajima-Morosuku (Tajima-Morosuke), from whom the name "Morosugi" derives, appears in both the 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' and is moreover identified as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, Morosugi Shrine is considered to have been established in ancient times{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. The Mizukami area, said to be the shrine's original site, remains part of Morosugi Shrine's parishioner territory to this day, and during festivals a portable shrine procession (mikoshi togyo) is conducted all the way to Mizukami{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}{{Sfn|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is recorded as "Morosugi Shrine / Morosugi Shrine" under {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} {{ill|lt=Izushi District|Q11395767|WD=Q11395767|1=Izushi District, Hyōgo}}, and is listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Subsequent history is not well documented, but as mentioned above, in Tenshō 2 (1574), when the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|WD=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} relocated from {{ill|lt=Konosumi-yama Castle|Q11545747|WD=Q11545747|1=Konosumiyama Castle}} to {{ill|lt=Ariko-yama Castle|Q11516348|WD=Q11516348|1=Arikoyama Castle}}, Morosugi Shrine was also transferred from Mizukami Village to its present location below the castle{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was enshrined north of the yamato-no-kuruwa (mountain villa enclosure) of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}{{Sfn|内町（平凡社）|1999年}}, and received reverence from the successive castle lords of the {{ill|lt=Koide clan|Q11459112|WD=Q11459112|1=Koide clan (Negi Daibu)}}, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira clan|Q1141077|WD=Q1141077|1=Matsudaira clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Sengoku clan|Q11378184|WD=Q11378184|1=Sengoku clan}}, who provided for shrine construction and donations of offerings. During the Sengoku clan's tenure as castle lords, it was customary for the lord himself to visit Morosugi Shrine upon returning from {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the Edo period, the shrine was also known as "Morosugi Daimyōjin"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and on April 21 of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. In the interim, the shrine buildings were destroyed by fire in Meiji 9 ({{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}}), but were rebuilt in Meiji 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}). === Precinct === * Honden - Three bays on the front, two bays on the rear, and two bays on the sides, in {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, with a chidori-hafu (cusped gable) on the front. The roof is clad in copper sheeting. * Haiden - irimoya-zukuri style with a tiled roof{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. * Mikoshi storehouse File:Morosugi-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Morosugi-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden === Auxiliary shrines === * Kawakami Shrine - Deity: Seoritsuhime-no-Kami * Tenjin Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shanichi Shrine * Nitta Shrine * Mihashira Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Inari Shrine * Ōkuni Shrine * Inari Shrine === Festivals === * Annual festival (Sunday before October 15) *: Formerly held on September 9, and later on October 15{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)|Q11587232|WD=Q11587232|lt=Isobe Shrine|1=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ifukube Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, a "danjiri matsuri" is held in which approximately 20 {{ill|lt=danjiri|Q11271630|WD=Q11271630|1=danjiri}} are crashed into each other in front of Izushi Castle in a fighting festival. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 '''Access''' * Approximately 4 minutes on foot southeast from {{ill|lt=Shinkoro|Q11637916|WD=Q11637916|1=Shinkorō}} in central Izushi '''Vicinity''' * {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Meiji Jinja Shiryō Compilation Office, ed.|author=|year=1912|chapter=Morosugi Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Jinja Shiryō Compilation Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|明治神社誌料|1912年}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/387}} 'Meiji Jinja Shiryō: Fuken Gōsha, Volume 1'] (National Diet Library Digital Collection), see frames 387-388. * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society, ed.|author={{ill|lt=Kuwahara Kiminori|Q126707715|WD=Q126707715|1=Tadanori Kuwabara}}|chapter=Morosugi Shrine|year=1984|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 19|publisher={{ill|lt=Kōgakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509|1=Japanese Historical Topography Series}} 29-1: Place Names of Hyōgo Prefecture, Volume 1|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=978-4582490619|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference="Uchimachi"|ref={{Harvid|内町（平凡社）|1999年}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Mizukami Village"|ref={{Harvid|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}}}. === See also === * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hiboko / Ame-no-Hihoko) * {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Morosugi-jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6323001.html Morosugi Shrine] - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Agency Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Three-bay irimoya-zukuri |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Mid-October (Autumn festival) |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Morosugi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Morosugi Shrine''' (もろすぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Izushi-chō Uchimachi, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is located at the northern foot of Ariko-yama, east of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}. === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deity is the following one kamiPrecinct information board.: * '''Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami''' (たじまもろすくのかみ) *: In the '{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}},' the name is written as "Tajima-Morosuku," and in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}},' as "Tajima-Morosuke." Both texts identify this deity as a child of {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hihoko). The shrine name "Morosugi" is said to derive from "Morosuku (Morosuke)"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. As noted above, the enshrined deity is regarded as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, the deity who opened up the Izushi area. approximately 20 {{ill|lt=danjiri|Q11271630|WD=Q11271630|1=danjiri}} are crashed into each other in front of Izushi Castle in a fighting festival. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * d'Izushi|ja|出石城|zh|出石城|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}} == TripAdvisor ID (P3134) == * 15051227 == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 3140005012627 == Commons category (P373) == * Morosugi-jinja == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '兵庫県豊岡市出石町内町28', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Morosugi-jinja}} [[JA:諸杉神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11632102}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Morosugi Shrine |画像 = [[File:Morosugi-jinja01s4592.jpg|280px]]
Torii and haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|27|38.34|N|134|52|30.43|E|region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Morosugi Shrine}} |祭神 = Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Three-bay irimoya-zukuri |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Mid-October (Autumn festival) |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Morosugi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Morosugi Shrine''' (もろすぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Izushi-chō Uchimachi, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is located at the northern foot of Ariko-yama, east of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}. === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deity is the following one kamiPrecinct information board.: * '''Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami''' (たじまもろすくのかみ) *: In the '{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}},' the name is written as "Tajima-Morosuku," and in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}},' as "Tajima-Morosuke." Both texts identify this deity as a child of {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hihoko). The shrine name "Morosugi" is said to derive from "Morosuku (Morosuke)"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. As noted above, the enshrined deity is regarded as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, the deity who opened up the Izushi area. Throughout the Izushi region, in addition to Morosugi Shrine, shrines of the Ame-no-Hiboko lineage are widely distributed, including {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Miyauchi), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mi-Izushi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Kirino), {{ill|lt=Nakashima Shrine|Q11364695|WD=Q11364695|1=Nakashima Shrine}} (Toyooka, Miyake), and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sugi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Araki){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown. According to shrine tradition, the shrine was originally located in Mizukami Village (present-day Toyooka, Izushi-chō Mizukami), near Izushi Shrine, and was transferred to its present location in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Since Tajima-Morosuku (Tajima-Morosuke), from whom the name "Morosugi" derives, appears in both the 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' and is moreover identified as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, Morosugi Shrine is considered to have been established in ancient times{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. The Mizukami area, said to be the shrine's original site, remains part of Morosugi Shrine's parishioner territory to this day, and during festivals a portable shrine procession (mikoshi togyo) is conducted all the way to Mizukami{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}{{Sfn|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is recorded as "Morosugi Shrine / Morosugi Shrine" under {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} {{ill|lt=Izushi District|Q11395767|WD=Q11395767|1=Izushi District, Hyōgo}}, and is listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Subsequent history is not well documented, but as mentioned above, in Tenshō 2 (1574), when the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|WD=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} relocated from {{ill|lt=Konosumi-yama Castle|Q11545747|WD=Q11545747|1=Konosumiyama Castle}} to {{ill|lt=Ariko-yama Castle|Q11516348|WD=Q11516348|1=Arikoyama Castle}}, Morosugi Shrine was also transferred from Mizukami Village to its present location below the castle{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was enshrined north of the yamato-no-kuruwa (mountain villa enclosure) of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}{{Sfn|内町（平凡社）|1999年}}, and received reverence from the successive castle lords of the {{ill|lt=Koide clan|Q11459112|WD=Q11459112|1=Koide clan (Negi Daibu)}}, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira clan|Q1141077|WD=Q1141077|1=Matsudaira clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Sengoku clan|Q11378184|WD=Q11378184|1=Sengoku clan}}, who provided for shrine construction and donations of offerings. During the Sengoku clan's tenure as castle lords, it was customary for the lord himself to visit Morosugi Shrine upon returning from {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the Edo period, the shrine was also known as "Morosugi Daimyōjin"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and on April 21 of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. In the interim, the shrine buildings were destroyed by fire in Meiji 9 ({{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}}), but were rebuilt in Meiji 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}). === Precinct === * Honden - Three bays on the front, two bays on the rear, and two bays on the sides, in {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, with a chidori-hafu (cusped gable) on the front. The roof is clad in copper sheeting. * Haiden - irimoya-zukuri style with a tiled roof{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. * Mikoshi storehouse File:Morosugi-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Morosugi-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden === Auxiliary shrines === * Kawakami Shrine - Deity: Seoritsuhime-no-Kami * Tenjin Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shanichi Shrine * Nitta Shrine * Mihashira Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Inari Shrine * Ōkuni Shrine * Inari Shrine === Festivals === * Annual festival (Sunday before October 15) *: Formerly held on September 9, and later on October 15{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)|Q11587232|WD=Q11587232|lt=Isobe Shrine|1=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ifukube Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, a "danjiri matsuri" is held in which approximately 20 {{ill|lt=danjiri|Q11271630|WD=Q11271630|1=danjiri}} are crashed into each other in front of Izushi Castle in a fighting festival. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 '''Access''' * Approximately 4 minutes on foot southeast from {{ill|lt=Shinkoro|Q11637916|WD=Q11637916|1=Shinkorō}} in central Izushi '''Vicinity''' * {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === Torii and haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|27|38.34|N|134|52|30.43|E|region:JP-28_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Morosugi Shrine}} |祭神 = Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Three-bay irimoya-zukuri |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Mid-October (Autumn festival) |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Morosugi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Morosugi Shrine''' (もろすぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Izushi-chō Uchimachi, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is located at the northern foot of Ariko-yama, east of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}. === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deity is the following one kamiPrecinct information board.: * '''Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami''' (たじまもろすくのかみ) *: In the '{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}},' the name is written as "Tajima-Morosuku," and in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}},' as "Tajima-Morosuke." Both texts identify this deity as a child of {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hihoko). The shrine name "Morosugi" is said to derive from "Morosuku (Morosuke)"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. As noted above, the enshrined deity is regarded as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, the deity who opened up the Izushi area. Throughout the Izushi region, in addition to Morosugi Shrine, shrines of the Ame-no-Hiboko lineage are widely distributed, including {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Miyauchi), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mi-Izushi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Kirino), {{ill|lt=Nakashima Shrine|Q11364695|WD=Q11364695|1=Nakashima Shrine}} (Toyooka, Miyake), and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sugi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Araki){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown. According to shrine tradition, the shrine was originally located in Mizukami Village (present-day Toyooka, Izushi-chō Mizukami), near Izushi Shrine, and was transferred to its present location in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Since Tajima-Morosuku (Tajima-Morosuke), from whom the name "Morosugi" derives, appears in both the 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' and is moreover identified as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, Morosugi Shrine is considered to have been established in ancient times{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. The Mizukami area, said to be the shrine's original site, remains part of Morosugi Shrine's parishioner territory to this day, and during festivals a portable shrine procession (mikoshi togyo) is conducted all the way to Mizukami{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}{{Sfn|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is recorded as "Morosugi Shrine / Morosugi Shrine" under {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} {{ill|lt=Izushi District|Q11395767|WD=Q11395767|1=Izushi District, Hyōgo}}, and is listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Subsequent history is not well documented, but as mentioned above, in Tenshō 2 (1574), when the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|WD=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} relocated from {{ill|lt=Konosumi-yama Castle|Q11545747|WD=Q11545747|1=Konosumiyama Castle}} to {{ill|lt=Ariko-yama Castle|Q11516348|WD=Q11516348|1=Arikoyama Castle}}, Morosugi Shrine was also transferred from Mizukami Village to its present location below the castle{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was enshrined north of the yamato-no-kuruwa (mountain villa enclosure) of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}{{Sfn|内町（平凡社）|1999年}}, and received reverence from the successive castle lords of the {{ill|lt=Koide clan|Q11459112|WD=Q11459112|1=Koide clan (Negi Daibu)}}, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira clan|Q1141077|WD=Q1141077|1=Matsudaira clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Sengoku clan|Q11378184|WD=Q11378184|1=Sengoku clan}}, who provided for shrine construction and donations of offerings. During the Sengoku clan's tenure as castle lords, it was customary for the lord himself to visit Morosugi Shrine upon returning from {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the Edo period, the shrine was also known as "Morosugi Daimyōjin"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and on April 21 of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. In the interim, the shrine buildings were destroyed by fire in Meiji 9 ({{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}}), but were rebuilt in Meiji 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}). === Precinct === * Honden - Three bays on the front, two bays on the rear, and two bays on the sides, in {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, with a chidori-hafu (cusped gable) on the front. The roof is clad in copper sheeting. * Haiden - irimoya-zukuri style with a tiled roof{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. * Mikoshi storehouse File:Morosugi-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Morosugi-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden === Auxiliary shrines === * Kawakami Shrine - Deity: Seoritsuhime-no-Kami * Tenjin Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shanichi Shrine * Nitta Shrine * Mihashira Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Inari Shrine * Ōkuni Shrine * Inari Shrine === Festivals === * Annual festival (Sunday before October 15) *: Formerly held on September 9, and later on October 15{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)|Q11587232|WD=Q11587232|lt=Isobe Shrine|1=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ifukube Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, a "danjiri matsuri" is held in which approximately 20 {{ill|lt=danjiri|Q11271630|WD=Q11271630|1=danjiri}} are crashed into each other in front of Izushi Castle in a fighting festival. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 '''Access''' * Approximately 4 minutes on foot southeast from {{ill|lt=Shinkoro|Q11637916|WD=Q11637916|1=Shinkorō}} in central Izushi '''Vicinity''' * {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === As noted above, the enshrined deity is regarded as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, the deity who opened up the Izushi area. Throughout the Izushi region, in addition to Morosugi Shrine, shrines of the Ame-no-Hiboko lineage are widely distributed, including {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Miyauchi), {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mi-Izushi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Kirino), {{ill|lt=Nakashima Shrine|Q11364695|WD=Q11364695|1=Nakashima Shrine}} (Toyooka, Miyake), and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sugi Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Toyooka, Izushi-chō Araki){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. === History === The date of founding is unknown. According to shrine tradition, the shrine was originally located in Mizukami Village (present-day Toyooka, Izushi-chō Mizukami), near Izushi Shrine, and was transferred to its present location in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}){{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Since Tajima-Morosuku (Tajima-Morosuke), from whom the name "Morosugi" derives, appears in both the 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' and is moreover identified as the legitimate heir of Ame-no-Hiboko, Morosugi Shrine is considered to have been established in ancient times{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. The Mizukami area, said to be the shrine's original site, remains part of Morosugi Shrine's parishioner territory to this day, and during festivals a portable shrine procession (mikoshi togyo) is conducted all the way to Mizukami{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}{{Sfn|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}' {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in {{ill|lt=Enchō (era)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}), the shrine is recorded as "Morosugi Shrine / Morosugi Shrine" under {{ill|lt=Tajima Province|Q1053727|WD=Q1053727|1=Tajima Province}} {{ill|lt=Izushi District|Q11395767|WD=Q11395767|1=Izushi District, Hyōgo}}, and is listed as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Subsequent history is not well documented, but as mentioned above, in Tenshō 2 (1574), when the {{ill|lt=Yamana clan|Q2975406|WD=Q2975406|1=Yamana clan}} relocated from {{ill|lt=Konosumi-yama Castle|Q11545747|WD=Q11545747|1=Konosumiyama Castle}} to {{ill|lt=Ariko-yama Castle|Q11516348|WD=Q11516348|1=Arikoyama Castle}}, Morosugi Shrine was also transferred from Mizukami Village to its present location below the castle{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, it was enshrined north of the yamato-no-kuruwa (mountain villa enclosure) of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}{{Sfn|内町（平凡社）|1999年}}, and received reverence from the successive castle lords of the {{ill|lt=Koide clan|Q11459112|WD=Q11459112|1=Koide clan (Negi Daibu)}}, {{ill|lt=Matsudaira clan|Q1141077|WD=Q1141077|1=Matsudaira clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Sengoku clan|Q11378184|WD=Q11378184|1=Sengoku clan}}, who provided for shrine construction and donations of offerings. During the Sengoku clan's tenure as castle lords, it was customary for the lord himself to visit Morosugi Shrine upon returning from {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. During the Edo period, the shrine was also known as "Morosugi Daimyōjin"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in October of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and on April 21 of {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174|1=Taishō era}} 13 ({{ill|lt=1924|Q2173|WD=Q2173|1=1924}}), it was elevated to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. In the interim, the shrine buildings were destroyed by fire in Meiji 9 ({{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766|1=1876}}), but were rebuilt in Meiji 17 ({{ill|lt=1884|Q7819|WD=Q7819|1=1884}}). === Precinct === * Honden - Three bays on the front, two bays on the rear, and two bays on the sides, in {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} style, with a chidori-hafu (cusped gable) on the front. The roof is clad in copper sheeting. * Haiden - irimoya-zukuri style with a tiled roof{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. * Mikoshi storehouse File:Morosugi-jinja honden.JPG|Honden File:Morosugi-jinja haiden.JPG|Haiden === Auxiliary shrines === * Kawakami Shrine - Deity: Seoritsuhime-no-Kami * Tenjin Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine * Shanichi Shrine * Nitta Shrine * Mihashira Shrine * Hachiman Shrine * Inari Shrine * Ōkuni Shrine * Inari Shrine === Festivals === * Annual festival (Sunday before October 15) *: Formerly held on September 9, and later on October 15{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}. Together with {{ill|lt=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)|Q11587232|WD=Q11587232|lt=Isobe Shrine|1=Isobe Shrine (Toyooka)}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ifukube Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, a "danjiri matsuri" is held in which approximately 20 {{ill|lt=danjiri|Q11271630|WD=Q11271630|1=danjiri}} are crashed into each other in front of Izushi Castle in a fighting festival. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}} Izushi-chō Uchimachi 28 '''Access''' * Approximately 4 minutes on foot southeast from {{ill|lt=Shinkoro|Q11637916|WD=Q11637916|1=Shinkorō}} in central Izushi '''Vicinity''' * {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Meiji Jinja Shiryō Compilation Office, ed.|author=|year=1912|chapter=Morosugi Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Jinja Shiryō Compilation Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|明治神社誌料|1912年}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/387}} 'Meiji Jinja Shiryō: Fuken Gōsha, Volume 1'] (National Diet Library Digital Collection), see frames 387-388. * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society, ed.|author={{ill|lt=Kuwahara Kiminori|Q126707715|WD=Q126707715|1=Tadanori Kuwabara}}|chapter=Morosugi Shrine|year=1984|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 19|publisher={{ill|lt=Kōgakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584|1=Kogakkan University}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Village"|ref={{Harvid|水上村（平凡社）|1999年}}}}. === See also === * {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hiboko / Ame-no-Hihoko) * {{ill|lt=Izushi Shrine|Q3156511|WD=Q3156511|1=Izushi Shrine}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Morosugi-jinja}} * [https://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6323001.html Morosugi Shrine] - Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Agency Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Three-bay irimoya-zukuri |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Mid-October (Autumn festival) |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Morosugi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Morosugi Shrine''' (もろすぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Izushi-chō Uchimachi, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. It is located at the northern foot of Ariko-yama, east of {{ill|lt=Izushi Castle|Q2968296|WD=Q2968296|1=Izushi Castle}}. === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deity is the following one kamiPrecinct information board.: * '''Tajima-Morosuku-no-Kami''' (たじまもろすくのかみ) *: In the '{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}},' the name is written as "Tajima-Morosuku," and in the '{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}},' as "Tajima-Morosuke." Both texts identify this deity as a child of {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Hiboko|Q713100|WD=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}} (Ame-no-Hihoko). The shrine name "Morosugi" is said to derive from "Morosuku (Morosuke)"{{Sfn|諸杉神社（式内社）|1984年}}.
